retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, RNA Structure -- Function -- MicroRNA
retrieved_docs.content: MicroRNAs (miRNA) are non-coding RNAs mainly involved in gene regulation. They are mostly processed from introns and are transcribed into primary miRNA from the host gene by RNA polymerase II. They are then modified by endonucleases, such as Drosha and Dicer, into mature miRNA. Studies have shown that miRNAs that bind to an untranslated region (3’UTR) on mRNAs suppress translation, while miRNA binding to promoter regions can upregulate transcription. [13] miRNAs can also function similarly to hormones. They are released into the extracellular fluid and taken up by target cells for regulation of cellular activity. Additionally, researchers are studying these extracellular miRNAs as ideal biomarkers for various diseases. Research has already shown circulating miRNAs to be involved in cancer through their role in controlling oncogenes and tumor suppressors. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Patisiran -- Mechanism of Action -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: This RISC separates the strands of the siRNA sequence. One strand releases and the other remains bound to the RISC. Then, the bound strand of siRNA functions as a targeting sequence for the complementary mRNA sequence. The bound patisiran strand binds to the genetically conserved sequence located in the 3' untranslated region of the complementary mRNA encoding abnormal transthyretin and the alignment of the RISC complex with it. [1] Subsequently, transthyretin mRNA is cleaved to render it nonfunctional. The degradation of mRNA coding wild-type and mutant transthyretin, a process of post-transcriptional gene silencing, results in reduced levels of TTR protein in the serum and, ultimately, deposition of TTR protein, also called amyloid, in tissues. [2] [14] One copy of the siRNA target sequence can destroy multiple complementary transthyretin mRNA.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Historically, pseudogenes were referred to as "junk DNA" due to their location in non-coding sequences of the genome. However, recent studies have begun to unravel various functions of pseudogenes and their mRNA transcripts. These functions vary in regulatory roles, and they include antisense RNA, siRNA, miRNA-like, and miRNA binding or inhibiting properties.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemical Markers of Osteoporosis -- Procedures -- MicroRNAs
retrieved_docs.content: Serum and plasma samples can be analyzed for miRNAs using various methods, including GeneChip™ miRNA Array, real-time polymerase chain reaction (PCR), next-generation sequencing (NGS), and OsteomiR ® RUO assay. [32] The GeneChip™ miRNA Array profiles multiple miRNAs simultaneously, while real-time PCR quantifies specific miRNA targets. NGS provides a comprehensive analysis of the entire miRNA transcriptome, offering insights into both known and novel miRNAs involved in bone turnover processes. [33] For a more targeted approach, the OsteomiR ® RUO assay is designed to quantify 19 specific miRNAs related to bone metabolism, making it a valuable tool for researching osteoporotic conditions and bone health. Each method enables precise miRNA expression measurement, offering the potential for more personalized and targeted diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in bone health management. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Another mechanism of pseudogene function lies in its ability to affect mRNA stability through interactions with miRNA.  miRNA typically pairs with the 3’ untranslated region of an mRNA transcript and causes degradation or lower expression levels. One example of how pseudogene mRNA transcripts interact with miRNA is in the relationship between PTEN, a tumor suppressor, and its pseudogene PTENP1. In one study, the PTENP1 pseudogene mRNA binds to miRNA and has decreased the concentration of the functional miRNA within the cell. In this way, PTENP1 allows the PTEN mRNA to escape miRNA repression.  Another study is exploring the regulatory relationship between heat shock proteins, Hsp90, and its associated pseudogenes HSP90AA1 and HSP90AA2. Heat shock proteins actively express and account for two percent of all expressed proteins. These proteins, through microarray data, have numerous retrotransposed pseudogenes. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Kuru -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prion proteins (PrP) are encoded by the prion protein gene (PRNP gene). [13] PrPc is a normally folded protein, rich in alpha-helices, and undergoes complete proteolysis. The PrPSc isoform is the misfolded protein that gives rise to the disease; it is rich in beta sheets and is resistant to degradation by enzymes. [14] Thus the PrPSc aggregates and further promotes the conversion of other PrPc to PrPSc. A chain reaction is set, which results in the pathogenesis of prion disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen is a 20-base pair, 2’-O-methoxyethyl modified antisense oligonucleotide complementary to human apolipoprotein B-100 messenger RNA. [3] Mipomersen aims to identify and hybridize with the human apolipoprotein B-100 mRNA via the traditional deoxyribonucleotide or ribonucleotide base pairing mechanism. The interface between the complementary mipomersen therapeutic oligonucleotide with the newly synthesized human apolipoprotein B-100 leads to the degradation of the messenger RNA. Most frequently, the hybridization of mipomersen to its target mRNA leads to the recruitment of RNase H1, a catalytic enzyme that degrades ribonucleic acid in the cellular cytoplasm. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Messengers -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Although abnormal production is the most common mechanism of disease, it is not the only one; other mechanisms involve different stages of the signaling process; as we saw before, the signaling process entails production, secretion, and reception of the messenger; disruption of any of these processes lead to alteration in signaling.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemical Markers of Osteoporosis -- Introduction -- MicroRNAs
retrieved_docs.content: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small, noncoding RNAs that regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally and play an essential role in various biological processes, including bone metabolism. Emerging research on miRNAs presents a promising opportunity to enhance the diagnosis and management of osteoporosis. Circulating miRNAs in serum have been shown to correlate with bone metabolism and osteoporosis. These miRNAs influence the differentiation and activity of osteoblasts and osteoclasts, thereby affecting bone formation and resorption. Moreover, commercial panels such as OsteomiR ® , which assess a set of 19 bone-related miRNAs, highlight their potential for clinical application. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: On a molecular level, pseudogenes have associations with several roles. Numerous studies have revealed that specific genes and their associated pseudogenes display regulatory roles in the cell. Some pseudogenes have exhibited antisense RNA properties, siRNA properties, and even an ability to affect mRNA stability. Through these molecular functions, pseudogene transcripts can modulate the number of parental copy transcripts expressed.

retrieved_docs.title: Patisiran -- Mechanism of Action -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Patisiran, a novel treatment for polyneuropathy associated with hATTR, is a small, double-stranded interfering RNA molecule (ALN-18328) formulated in a lipid covering using micro nanotechnology. [15] [16] This nanoparticle also contains 2 novel lipid excipients (PEG-C-DMG and DLin-MC3-DMA) to facilitate siRNA entry into the target cell cytoplasm. [17] Patisiran is administered via intravenous infusion and transported to the liver by blood. The apolipoprotein opsonized in the lipid capsule of the nanoparticle is recognized by the apolipoprotein-E receptors of hepatocytes, and the drug internalizes by endocytosis. The disruption of the nanoparticles in hepatocytes releases the pharmacologically active moiety siRNA. Then, siRNA processing by the ribonuclease, also known as the Dicer enzyme, occurs. This process involves cleaving the overhanging nucleotides on the edges of the siRNA. The processed siRNA binds to the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC).

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemical Markers of Osteoporosis -- Specimen Collection -- Bone Turnover Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: When using the OsteomiR ® RUO assay for miRNA analysis, a starting sample size of 200 μL of plasma is recommended to ensure adequate RNA yield. After thawing and centrifuging the plasma samples for 5 minutes, synthetic RNA spike-in is mixed with the lysis buffer provided in the assay kit. RNA is then precipitated out of the solution with 100% isopropanol and glycogen. Finally, the OsteomiR ® chemistry kit is used to perform reverse transcription and PCR amplification. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Adrenocortical Cancer -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small RNAs (approximately 22 nucleotides) regulating gene expression at the posttranscriptional level by targeting mRNAs for cleavage or translation repression. They play an essential role in the pathogenesis of several neoplasms; they are responsible for both oncogene activation and tumor suppressor genes silencing. Their profile of expression can serve as diagnostic and/or prognosis markers and the fact that one can measure them in blood samples. [20] In ACC deregulation of several miRNAs are known to occur. The expression of miRNAs seems to differ significantly between adrenocortical adenomas and adrenocortical carcinomas. Several miRNAs received particular focus, the most frequently reported being the downregulation of miR-335 and miR-195 and upregulation of miR-483-5p. [21] The level of circulating miR-483-5p allows for the distinction between ACC and benign tumors. [20] [22] In childhood adrenocortical tumors, a set of miRNAs harbors a differential expression in comparison with normal adrenal tissue. These miRNAs are mainly downregulated (miR-99a, miR-100); however, the upregulation of miR-483 occurs in both childhood ACC and adult ACC. The profile of miRNA expression shows three clusters in ACC associated with different prognosis. [21] .  In particular, miR-483-3p correlates inversely with the expression of the pro-apoptotic protein PUMA (p53 upregulated modulator of apoptosis) suggesting a role for this miR in apoptosis regulation. [21] The gene encoding miR-483- 5p is in the IGF2 locus, and the level of IGF2 expression directly correlates with this miRNA. [23] The miR-99a and miR-100 participate in the regulation of mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) signaling, a pathway involved in cellular proliferation in adrenocortical carcinoma. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: EVs also carry miRNAs that repress tumor suppression proteins. Additionally, EVs represent a mechanism through which cells can discard tumor suppressor signals, effectively down-regulating them and allowing for cell proliferation.  The most studied roles of EVs in cancer include cell migration, invasion, and metastasis.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Epigenetic Mechanism -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: The most recently elucidated epigenetic mechanism is non-coding RNA-associated gene silencing. A non-coding RNA (ncRNA) is a functional RNA molecule that is transcribed but not translated into proteins. Once regarded as waste of the genome, recent insight suggests the ncRNA molecules harbor a crucial role in epigenetic gene expression and likely account for the great difference in phenotype between species and within human populations despite such similarity in encoded proteins. [6] [18] Notable ncRNA molecules include microRNAs (miRNA) and short interfering RNAs (siRNA), which include less than 30 nucleotides, and long non-coding RNAs (lncRNA), which are 200 nucleotides or longer. Though the full extent of their role in epigenetics is still being determined, there is evidence suggesting that ncRNAs participate in DNA methylation and histone modifications in addition to gene silencing. [20] siRNAs and lncRNAs both have been shown to regulate gene expression by the formation of heterochromatin. [6] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Trinucleotide Repeat Disorders -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Treatments That Limit the Expression of Trinucleotide Repeats
retrieved_docs.content: Polyglutamate-related disorders, such as Huntington disease and SCA, are at the forefront of trinucleotide repeat disorder research. These efforts aim to lower target mRNA levels either nonspecifically or in an allele-specific manner. Therapeutic agents, including microRNAs (miRNA) or ASOs, inhibit mRNA expression by binding to CUG repeat RNA, thereby blocking pathogenic RNA-protein interactions. This process releases expanded CUG transcripts from nuclear foci, allowing their transport to the cytoplasm for rapid degradation. This reduction in mRNA levels decreases aberrant protein production and cytotoxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 0 -- Community 15
retrieved_docs.content: associated with increased blood and tissue alkalinity) and nicotinic acid and its derivatives (chemical class nicofuranose, possessing lipid‑modifying and peripheral vasodilatory actions). “Other lipid‑modifying agents in ATC” comprise mipomersen (which lowers lipids by decreasing protein synthesis), inclisiran (small‑interfering RNA), ezetimib, bempedoic acid, alirocumab, evolocumab, and lomitapide; the benzimidazole heterocyclic scaffold underlies both dabigatran etexilate (an oral direct thrombin inhibitor) and lomitapide, illustrating how a single chemical core can support diverse therapeutic classes. Direct thrombin inhibitors such as argatroban and dabigatran etexilate prevent clot formation by directly inhibiting thrombin. Policosanol and tiadenol are related to fatty alcohols, which are chemically linked to the nutraceutical mixture policosanol. Cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers affect the pharmacology of klobazam by inducing the enzyme, and flibanserin is contraindicated with such

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 252
retrieved_docs.content: is a subclass of blood‑forming organ drugs and encompasses the more specific group “vitamin K and other hemostatics,” which includes agents that promote clot formation through vitamin K‑dependent pathways. Together, these interrelated classifications illustrate how antithrombotic, hemostatic, and enzymatic agents are organized within the pharmacological landscape of blood and blood‑forming organ therapeutics, providing a structured framework for understanding their mechanisms and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2296
retrieved_docs.content: Antifibrinolytics are a class of medications that inhibit the enzymatic breakdown of fibrin clots, thereby promoting hemostasis in situations of excessive bleeding. Vitamin K 1 (phylloquinone) and vitamin K 2 (menaquinone) are distinct forms of the vitamin K family that serve as essential cofactors for the γ‑carboxylation of several clotting factors, a process required for normal coagulation. Although both antifibrinolytics and the vitamin K compounds influence the coagulation system, antifibrinolytics are not subtypes of vitamin K 1 or vitamin K 2; they act through a different mechanism by directly blocking plasminogen activation rather than by providing the vitamin K‑dependent enzymatic support needed for clot factor maturation. Recognizing this distinction is important for patient care, as antifibrinolytic agents such as tranexamic acid are used to control acute hemorrhage, whereas vitamin K 1 or vitamin K 2 supplementation is employed to correct vitamin K deficiency–related bleeding disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2246
retrieved_docs.content: Tromethamin is categorized within the therapeutic class of Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders, indicating that it functions as a volume‑expanding agent used to restore or maintain intravascular fluid volume. This classification reflects its role in clinical situations such as hemorrhage, hypovolemia, or other conditions requiring rapid hemodynamic support, where augmenting plasma volume is essential. Recognizing tromethamin as part of the Blood Products/Modifiers/Volume Expanders class helps clinicians select appropriate volume‑expanding therapies and understand its mechanism of modifying blood components to increase circulatory volume.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2188
retrieved_docs.content: Antifibrinolytics are classified as a type of vitamin K₁, the fat‑soluble vitamin that is essential for the activation of several clotting factors and thus plays a critical role in the coagulation cascade. By being identified as vitamin K₁, antifibrinolytics are understood to contribute to hemostasis not only by directly inhibiting the breakdown of fibrin clots but also by supporting the vitamin K‑dependent pathways that promote clot formation. This dual action underscores their therapeutic relevance in conditions where enhanced clot stability is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitory enzymes have a broader relationship with protein C, indicating that protein C is subject to regulation by a variety of inhibitory enzymatic mechanisms. This broad interaction underscores the importance of multiple inhibitory pathways in modulating protein C activity, which is central to the control of coagulation and may inform therapeutic strategies targeting hemostatic balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K antagonists constitute a pharmacologic class of anticoagulant agents that inhibit the activity of vitamin K–dependent clotting factors, thereby preventing thrombus formation in a variety of clinical settings. This class is a subset of the broader antikoagulancia (anticoagulant) category, reflecting its role in blood‑thinning therapy. Representative drugs that belong to the Vitamin K antagonist class include fluindione, acenokumarol, ethylbiskumacetát, fenindion, fenprokumon, and warfarin (specifically warfarin sodium). Each of these agents shares the common mechanism of antagonizing vitamin K, leading to reduced synthesis of functional clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. While the triple linking becaplermin to antikoagulancia appears contradictory—becaplermin is a recombinant platelet‑derived growth factor used for wound healing rather than an anticoagulant—this relationship is likely erroneous and does not alter the established hierarchy. Consequently, the accurate medical understanding is that Vitamin K antagonists are a defined subgroup of antikoagulancia, and the listed compounds are individual members of the Vitamin K antagonist class, all of which are employed to manage and prevent thromboembolic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: Adenosine and acetylcholine are both classified as neurotransmitters, meaning they serve as chemical messengers that transmit signals across synapses in the nervous system. As neurotransmitters, each plays distinct but complementary roles in modulating neuronal activity, influencing processes such as sleep regulation, cardiovascular function, muscle contraction, and cognitive function. Recognizing that adenosine and acetylcholine share this broader classification helps clinicians understand their contributions to normal physiology and to various disorders, guiding therapeutic strategies that target neurotransmitter pathways for conditions like arrhythmias, neurodegenerative diseases, and sleep disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2360
retrieved_docs.content: Alfa-2-antiplasmin is classified as an antifibrinolytic, meaning it functions to inhibit fibrinolysis by neutralizing plasmin activity and thereby helping regulate clot breakdown.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 473
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen, inclisiran, evolocumab, policosanol, and tiadenol are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” Mipomersen exerts its lipid‑lowering effect by decreasing protein synthesis, specifically reducing apolipoprotein B production. Inclisiran and evolocumab also belong to this class but act through distinct mechanisms: inclisiran is a small interfering RNA that silences PCSK9 expression, while evolocumab is a monoclonal antibody that inhibits PCSK9 activity. Policosanol and tiadenol are fatty‑alcohol compounds, as indicated by their mapping to the term “mastné alkoholy” (fatty alcohols). Together, these agents illustrate the diversity of therapeutic strategies encompassed by the “Other lipid modifying agents” category, ranging from antisense oligonucleotides and RNA‑based therapies to monoclonal antibodies and fatty‑alcohol supplements, all aimed at managing dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2201
retrieved_docs.content: Factor VII is classified as a biologically active substance, meaning it belongs to the broader group of compounds that exert physiological effects in the body. Recognizing Factor VII as a biologically active substance highlights its role in essential processes such as the initiation of the coagulation cascade, where it contributes to blood clot formation and hemostasis. This classification aids clinicians and researchers in understanding the functional importance of Factor VII within the complex network of biologically active agents that regulate normal vascular and hemostatic function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1700
retrieved_docs.content: Hematological enzymes constitute a specialized group of enzymes that function within the blood and related tissues, and this group includes both hyaluronoglucosaminidase and trypsin. Hyaluronoglucosaminidase, an enzyme that degrades glycosaminoglycans such as hyaluronic acid, is classified as a member of the hematological enzyme family, reflecting its role in remodeling extracellular matrix components in the circulatory system. Similarly, trypsin, a serine protease traditionally known for its digestive functions, is also recognized as a member of the hematological enzymes, indicating its involvement in proteolytic processes that can occur in blood plasma and influence coagulation, inflammation, and other vascular activities. Together, these enzymes illustrate the diversity of biochemical activities encompassed by hematological enzymes and underscore their importance in maintaining normal blood physiology and responding to pathological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1339
retrieved_docs.content: BLOOD AND BLOOD FORMING ORGAN DRUGS constitute a broad therapeutic class that includes antithrombotic agents; antithrombotic agents are a specific subclass of these drugs and are used to inhibit platelet aggregation and coagulation, thereby preventing the formation of pathological blood clots in various clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 105
retrieved_docs.content: Antithrombotic agents are organized into distinct pharmacologic classes that differ in mechanism of action and therapeutic use. Direct Factor Xa inhibitors, exemplified by rivaroxaban, prevent thrombin generation by directly inhibiting factor Xa. Direct thrombin inhibitors, such as argatroban and dabigatran etexilate, block thrombin activity; dabigatran etexilate also incorporates a benzimidazole scaffold that contributes to its anticoagulant potency. The heparin group—including dalteparin and tinzaparin—exerts antithrombotic effects through potentiation of antithrombin III. Other antithrombotic agents classified in the ATC system comprise fondaparinux and dermatan sulfate. Vitamin‑K antagonists (VKAs) interfere with the vitamin‑K cycle, reducing synthesis of vitamin‑K‑dependent clotting factors; key members are warfarin sodium, fluindione, acenocoumarol, ethylbiskumacetate, fenindione, and fenprocoumon, with warfarin serving as the prototypical oral anticoagulant. Correct identification of these classes is essential for appropriate patient management and for avoiding confusion with agents outside the anticoagulant spectrum, such as lomitapide—a lipid‑lowering drug that inhibits microsomal triglyceride transfer protein and does not contain a benzimidazole moiety. Understanding the specific mechanisms, chemical scaffolds, and therapeutic categories of each agent supports accurate therapeutic decision‑making in the prevention and treatment of thrombotic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K is classified as a fat‑soluble vitamin, meaning it belongs to the broader group of vitamins that dissolve in lipids and require dietary fat for optimal absorption. This places vitamin K within the same category as vitamins A, D, and E, highlighting shared characteristics such as storage in fatty tissues and the need for adequate fat intake to ensure proper bioavailability. Recognizing vitamin K as a fat‑soluble vitamin is essential for understanding its absorption dynamics, its role in physiological processes like blood coagulation, and for guiding nutritional recommendations that support its effectiveness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K and other hemostatics produce hemostatické účinky, meaning they exert the physiological hemostatic effects that stop bleeding by promoting clot formation and maintaining vascular integrity. These hemostatic actions are central to the therapeutic role of vitamin K and related agents in controlling hemorrhage and supporting normal coagulation processes.
